one_paragraph_verdict: The article is a standard news report on geopolitical competition and trade policy, specifically focusing on China's export controls on rare earth materials to hinder US chipmaking. It does not engage with the theoretical distinctions between rent and profit, exploitation and expropriation, or the specific Marxist categories of accumulation that the theory map requires. While it mentions the state's role in regulating exports, it does not analyze the state's constitutive role in building the industry's market position or the mode of surplus extraction. Consequently, it fails all five relevance gates (G1-G5), as it provides only topical overlap regarding 'tech' and 'state' without any analytical engagement with the theory's mechanisms.
what_article_cannot_adjudicate: Whether tech R&D spending is productive capital investment or rent-seeking moat-building (C1)
Whether the state constitutively built the market dominance of specific firms rather than merely regulating trade (C3)
Whether tech firm profit rates behave like capitalist profits or monopoly rents (C4)
The ratio of tangible to intangible assets on tech balance sheets (C5)
The validity of the techno-feudalism diagnosis versus an expanded theory of capitalism (C6)
state_capital_nexus_relevance: The article describes the state acting as a geopolitical regulator of exports, which is a standard function of national security policy and does not provide evidence of the state's constitutive role in the internal capital accumulation or market dominance of specific firms as required by C3.
